title: Spectra of metric graphs and crystalline measures
speaker: Peter Sarnak
abstract: The geometric optics trace formula gives the singular support of wave trace on a compact Riemannian manifold. In the case of of a one dimensional singular manifold, that is a metric (or quantum) graph, this formula is exact and yields a crystalline measure generalizing the Poisson and related Summation Formulae.

proof_stages.label: Wave trace and crystalline-measure question
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames spectra through the wave trace of a compact Laplacian, recalls that singularities occur at periodic-orbit lengths, and motivates the question of when the spectral measure is crystalline.

proof_stages.label: Metric graph Laplacian and stable secular determinant
proof_stages.summary: A metric graph is given edge lengths and Kirchhoff-Neumann matching conditions, and its spectrum is encoded as the real zeros of a multivariable secular determinant whose stability follows from a unitary scattering matrix.

proof_stages.label: Irreducibility and subtori of the secular hypersurface
proof_stages.summary: The secular hypersurface is analyzed algebraically: after explicit loop factors, the remaining polynomial is irreducible except for specified exceptional graphs, and codimension-one translated subtori are thereby controlled.

proof_stages.label: Lang G_m intersection machinery
proof_stages.summary: Vertical, horizontal, and division-group forms of Lang's theorem for algebraic tori are invoked to say that intersections of a subvariety with torsion or finitely generated multiplicative subgroups come only from finitely many translated subtori plus finitely many residual points.

proof_stages.label: Linear/nonlinear spectral decomposition
proof_stages.summary: Applying the torus-intersection structure to the secular hypersurface along the exponential line of edge lengths splits the spectrum into arithmetic-progressive linear pieces and a nonlinear remainder with positive density, infinite rational span, and uniformly bounded intersections with any full arithmetic progression.

proof_stages.label: Periodic-orbit trace formula and new crystalline measures
proof_stages.summary: The Fourier transform of the graph spectral measure is computed as a sum of delta masses at closed-walk lengths weighted by primitive length and scattering phases, proving that metric graphs produce tempered crystalline measures and, in many cases, positive or idempotent non-Dirac-comb examples.

examples.name: Circle spectrum and Poisson comb
examples.note: The unit circle has integer frequencies, so its spectral measure Fourier-transforms to another picket fence, motivating the definition and search for crystalline measures.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes the circle eigenfunctions/eigenvalues and identifies the spectral delta comb with its Fourier-transform Poisson comb.

passes.reason: The circle is the minimal smooth one-dimensional case and the Poisson comb is a perfectly periodic self-dual lattice, used as the basic model.

examples.name: Figure-eight metric graph
examples.note: Its secular polynomial factors into three torus equations, visibly explaining how translated subtori produce arithmetic progressions in the spectrum, even though the figure eight is also a named exceptional case.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the figure-eight graph, giving its three arithmetic progressions and computing/factorizing its secular polynomial as z1-1, z2-1, z1z2-1.

passes.reason: The one-vertex two-loop bouquet is a low-complexity boundary graph whose factorized secular polynomial is presented as the obstruction to generic nonlinear behavior.
the figure-eight is a collapsed two-loop bouquet whose secular variety becomes linear, used as the unique exception to generic irreducibility/nonlinearity
the figure-eight is a collapsed single-vertex bouquet whose secular variety becomes a union of subtori and is used as the unique exception to generic nonlinearity

examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the difference of zero measures for two Dirichlet L-functions and writes its explicit-formula Fourier transform in terms of conductors and prime powers.

examples.name: Roots of unity on an algebraic curve in a torus
examples.note: A curve in a two-dimensional algebraic torus and its torsion points illustrate the vertical Lang theorem through the tension between large Galois orbits and upper bounds for rational points.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the roots-of-unity-in-a-torus situation by considering a curve/subvariety, Galois orbits of torsion points, and counting constraints leading to Lang’s theorem.
